text: What is the main purpose of this lecture?
choices: To explain how food production contributes to carbon emissions
To persuade students to become vegetarian
To compare the nutritional value of different diets
To describe how supermarkets reduce waste
explanation: The professor opens by stating the lecture will cover how the food we eat leaves a carbon footprint, and the entire talk develops that theme.
wrongReasons: B: vegetarianism is mentioned as one implication but is not the lecture's purpose
C: nutrition is never discussed
D: supermarket waste is not mentioned

passage.title: Lecture: The Carbon Footprint of Food
passage.body: Professor: Most people think about carbon emissions in terms of cars or factories, but today I want to focus on something closer to home — the food on your plate. Every item you eat has what we call a carbon footprint: the total greenhouse gases released during its production, transport, and disposal. Red meat, especially beef, has the largest footprint of any common food. Producing one kilogram of beef releases roughly 27 kilograms of CO₂-equivalent gases, largely because cattle produce methane and require enormous amounts of land and water. Vegetables and legumes, by contrast, emit a fraction of that. Then there is transport — flying fresh strawberries from Chile to Germany in winter adds significantly to their footprint. And food waste is a major factor too; roughly one third of all food produced globally is never eaten, meaning those emissions were entirely pointless. So when we talk about reducing our personal carbon footprint, diet is one of the most powerful levers we have.

text: According to the professor, approximately how much CO₂-equivalent is released per kilogram of beef?
choices: 3 kilograms
10 kilograms
27 kilograms
50 kilograms
explanation: The professor explicitly states 'producing one kilogram of beef releases roughly 27 kilograms of CO₂-equivalent gases.'
wrongReasons: A: 3 kg is not mentioned
B: 10 kg is not mentioned
D: 50 kg is not mentioned

text: What is the professor's attitude toward food waste?
choices: Resigned — it is an unavoidable part of modern agriculture
Critical — the associated emissions are needlessly produced
Optimistic — new technologies will soon eliminate it
Neutral — it is just one small factor among many
explanation: The professor calls the emissions from wasted food 'entirely pointless,' signalling clear criticism rather than resignation or optimism.
wrongReasons: A: the professor does not frame it as unavoidable
C: no technology solution is mentioned
D: the word 'pointless' conveys strong negative judgment, not neutrality

text: What is the main topic of the conversation?
choices: How the student can better manage academic stress
Why the student failed a recent examination
How to apply for a medical leave of absence
Which courses the student should drop
explanation: The advisor opens by asking about the student's stress levels, and the whole conversation focuses on strategies for coping with academic pressure.
wrongReasons: B: no exam failure is mentioned
C: medical leave is not discussed
D: dropping courses is never proposed

passage.title: Campus Conversation: Managing Academic Stress
passage.body: Advisor: Hi, come in. You mentioned in your email that things have been feeling overwhelming lately. Can you tell me more? Student: Yeah, I've got three major deadlines this week, and I honestly don't know where to start. I haven't been sleeping well either. Advisor: That's a really common pattern — when stress peaks, sleep suffers, and then everything feels harder. Have you tried breaking those deadlines down into smaller daily tasks? Student: Not really. I usually just stare at the whole project and panic. Advisor: That's the problem right there. Big tasks feel unmanageable until you chunk them. Try listing every single step needed — even tiny ones like 'open document' — and tick them off. The sense of progress helps. Student: What about the sleep side? Advisor: Avoid screens for an hour before bed, keep a consistent wake time even on weekends, and if your mind races at night, write your worries down on paper before you sleep. It externalises the anxiety. Student: Okay, that actually sounds doable. Advisor: Good. Come back and see me next week and we'll check in.

text: What specific sleep advice does the advisor give?
choices: Take a short nap every afternoon
Avoid screens for an hour before bed and keep a consistent wake time
Use sleeping pills if the mind races at night
Sleep for at least nine hours each night
explanation: The advisor explicitly lists: avoid screens an hour before bed, keep a consistent wake time, and write worries down. The first two match choice B.
wrongReasons: A: naps are not mentioned
C: medication is never suggested
D: a specific hour count is not given

choices: Writing makes the student share problems with others
Moving worries onto paper frees the mind from holding them
The student should show the written list to a counsellor
Writing increases awareness of how many worries one has
explanation: To externalise means to move something outside oneself; in this context the advisor means transferring mental worries to paper so the brain no longer has to hold onto them, reducing nighttime racing thoughts.
wrongReasons: A: sharing with others is not implied
C: showing the list to anyone is not mentioned
D: awareness of quantity is not the point being made

text: What is the lecture mainly about?
choices: The stages of sleep and why dreaming occurs
How the brain consolidates memories during sleep
The effects of sleep deprivation on academic performance
How to improve sleep quality through behavioural changes
explanation: The professor explicitly states the lecture is about memory consolidation during sleep, and every part of the talk develops that concept.
wrongReasons: A: dreaming is not the focus; it is mentioned only in passing
C: sleep deprivation is not discussed
D: behavioural sleep tips are not given

passage.title: Lecture: How Memory Consolidation Works During Sleep
passage.body: Professor: Today we're looking at one of the most fascinating functions of sleep — memory consolidation. When you learn something new during the day, the memory is initially fragile and stored in the hippocampus, a region of the brain associated with short-term storage. During sleep, especially during the slow-wave stages, the hippocampus essentially replays those memories and transfers them to the neocortex for long-term storage. Think of it like moving files from a temporary folder to permanent storage on a hard drive. REM sleep — the stage where most dreaming occurs — also plays a role, particularly in consolidating emotional memories and procedural skills like riding a bike or playing an instrument. This is why sleep-deprived students often find that material they studied the night before feels hazier the next morning. The consolidation process simply didn't complete. One practical implication: studying then sleeping is far more effective than an all-night revision session, because the brain needs sleep to cement what it has learned.

text: According to the professor, where are new memories initially stored?
choices: The neocortex
The prefrontal cortex
The hippocampus
The amygdala
explanation: The professor states that newly learned memories 'are initially fragile and stored in the hippocampus.'
wrongReasons: A: the neocortex is the destination after consolidation, not initial storage
B: the prefrontal cortex is not mentioned
D: the amygdala is not mentioned

wrongReasons: A: morning review as a fix is not mentioned
C: no such distinction between skill types is made
D: permanent damage is never claimed

text: Why has the student come to office hours?
choices: To get help choosing between qualitative and quantitative research methods
To ask for an extension on the research paper deadline
To discuss feedback on a previously submitted draft
To learn how to format citations correctly
explanation: The student opens by saying he cannot decide between qualitative and quantitative approaches for his project, and the professor guides him through that choice.
wrongReasons: B: no extension request is made
C: no draft has been submitted
D: citations are never mentioned

passage.title: Office Hours: Choosing a Research Methodology
passage.body: Student: Professor, I'm stuck on the methodology section. I can't decide whether to go qualitative or quantitative for my project on student attitudes to online learning. Professor: Good question. What's your core research question? Student: I want to understand why students prefer or avoid online classes — the reasons behind it. Professor: In that case, qualitative is almost certainly the better fit. When you're trying to understand the 'why' behind human behaviour — motivations, feelings, interpretations — qualitative methods like interviews or focus groups give you depth that a survey never can. Student: But wouldn't a survey reach more people? Professor: It would, but you'd end up with numbers telling you how many students feel a certain way, not why they feel that way. Quantitative is great for measuring and comparing; qualitative is great for understanding and exploring. Since you haven't done prior research establishing what factors matter, you need the exploratory depth first. Student: That makes sense. So interviews? Professor: Probably semi-structured interviews — gives you a guide but lets the conversation go where it needs to. Aim for around twelve to fifteen participants. Student: Perfect, thanks.

text: What type of interviews does the professor recommend, and how many participants?
choices: Structured interviews with at least 30 participants
Unstructured interviews with 5 to 8 participants
Semi-structured interviews with around 12 to 15 participants
Focus groups with 20 to 25 participants
explanation: The professor explicitly recommends 'semi-structured interviews' and says to 'aim for around twelve to fifteen participants.'
wrongReasons: A: structured interviews and 30 participants are not mentioned
B: unstructured interviews and 5–8 participants are not mentioned
D: focus groups are mentioned as an example of qualitative methods generally, not the specific recommendation

choices: They are inappropriate for any student-related research
They would provide numbers but not the depth of explanation the project needs
They are more rigorous and should be combined with qualitative methods
They are only suitable for large-scale national studies
explanation: The professor acknowledges surveys reach more people but says they produce numbers about how many feel a way — not why — and that the project needs exploratory depth, making quantitative insufficient here.
wrongReasons: A: quantitative is not dismissed for all student research
C: mixed methods is not suggested
D: scale is not given as the criterion

text: What is the main purpose of the lecture?
choices: To trace the key stages in the development of the internet
To explain the technical protocols that power the web
To argue that the internet has had a negative effect on society
To compare the internet with earlier communication technologies
explanation: The professor says they will cover 'the key stages' in the history of the internet, and the lecture moves chronologically through those stages.
wrongReasons: B: technical protocols are only briefly named, not the focus
C: no negative argument is made
D: earlier technologies are mentioned only as brief context

passage.title: Lecture: The History of the Internet
passage.body: Professor: The internet is so embedded in our lives that it is easy to forget it is a relatively recent invention. Today I want to trace its key stages. It starts in 1969 with ARPANET, a US Defence Department project that linked four university computers together — the first network. Through the 1970s, email was invented and the TCP/IP protocol was developed, essentially creating the shared language that lets computers communicate. The 1980s saw the network expand to universities worldwide, and in 1989 Tim Berners-Lee proposed the World Wide Web — a system of linked documents running on top of the internet. Many people confuse the web with the internet, but the web is just one application that uses the internet infrastructure. The 1990s brought commercial internet access, web browsers, and the dot-com boom. By the 2000s, broadband replaced dial-up, social media emerged, and smartphones made internet access mobile. Today we are entering a phase of ubiquitous connectivity — the Internet of Things — where everyday objects from fridges to traffic lights are networked. The story is still being written.

text: Who proposed the World Wide Web, and in which year?
choices: Vint Cerf, in 1973
Tim Berners-Lee, in 1989
Bill Gates, in 1991
Tim Berners-Lee, in 1995
explanation: The professor states: 'in 1989 Tim Berners-Lee proposed the World Wide Web.'
wrongReasons: A: Vint Cerf is associated with TCP/IP, not the web
C: Bill Gates is not mentioned
D: 1995 is incorrect; the professor says 1989

Today we are entering a phase of ubiquitous connectivity — the Internet of Things — where everyday objects from fridges to traffic lights are networked. The story is still being written.

wrongReasons: A: ARPANET's military origin is stated, not corrected as a misconception

text: What is the main problem the two students are discussing?
choices: They disagree about how to divide the work in their group project
One student wants to drop the course
Their professor has given them conflicting instructions
They cannot agree on which topic to choose for the project
explanation: The conversation centres on Maya feeling she does too much work and Lior not completing his assigned sections, which is a disagreement over workload division.
wrongReasons: B: dropping the course is not mentioned
C: conflicting instructions from a professor are not mentioned
D: a topic has already been chosen; the dispute is about workload

passage.title: Campus Conversation: Group Project Disagreement
passage.body: Maya: Lior, can we talk? I feel like I've been doing most of the work on this project and I'm really frustrated. Lior: What do you mean? I did the introduction. Maya: You did the introduction two days after the deadline we agreed on, and I ended up rewriting half of it. Lior: I had a lot going on with other assignments. Maya: I get that, but so did I. We agreed to split the work equally and that's not what's happening. Lior: Okay, look — what's left to do? Maya: The data analysis section and the conclusion. Lior: I'll do the conclusion — it's shorter. Maya: That's the problem right there. I don't want you to take the easier part again. We should split the data analysis together. Lior: Fine. But I need you to actually tell me when you think something isn't working, instead of just redoing it yourself and then being angry later. Maya: You're right, I should have said something sooner. So — data analysis together, you draft the conclusion, I review it, and we meet Thursday to put it all together? Lior: Deal.

text: What specific criticism does Maya raise about Lior's introduction?
choices: It was too short and lacked references
It was submitted late and she had to rewrite half of it
It did not follow the format agreed with the professor
It was copied from another source
explanation: Maya says Lior submitted the introduction 'two days after the deadline we agreed on' and that she 'ended up rewriting half of it.'
wrongReasons: A: length and references are not mentioned
C: format problems are not raised
D: plagiarism is not mentioned

passage.title: Campus Conversation: Group Project Disagreement
passage.body: Maya: Lior, can we talk? I feel like I've been doing most of the work on this project and I'm really frustrated. Lior: What do you mean? I did the introduction. Maya: You did the introduction two days after the deadline we agreed on, and I ended up rewriting half of it. Lior: I had a lot going on with other assignments. Maya: I get that, but so did I. We agreed to split the work equally and that's not what's happening. Lior: Okay, look — what's left to do? Maya: The data analysis section and the conclusion. Lior: I'll do the conclusion — it's shorter. Maya: That's the problem right there. I don't want you to take the easier part again. We should split the data analysis together. Lior: Fine. But I need you to actually tell me when you think something isn't working, instead of just redoing it yourself and then being angry later. Maya: You're right, I should have said something sooner. So — data analysis together, you draft the conclusion, I review it, and we meet Thursday to put it all together? Lior: Deal.

text: What does Lior suggest Maya should change about her behaviour?
choices: She should contribute more to the written sections
She should communicate problems earlier rather than silently redoing work
She should speak to the professor about the workload distribution
She should set firmer internal deadlines for the group
explanation: Lior tells Maya she should 'actually tell me when you think something isn't working, instead of just redoing it yourself and then being angry later.' Maya agrees.
wrongReasons: A: Lior does not say Maya contributes too little
C: involving the professor is not suggested
D: firmer deadlines are not Lior's suggestion

So — data analysis together, you draft the conclusion, I review it, and we meet Thursday to put it all together? Lior: Deal.

text: What does Maya's response 'you're right, I should have said something sooner' reveal?
choices: She realises she is primarily responsible for the project's problems
She acknowledges that her habit of silent correction contributed to the breakdown in communication
She is trying to end the argument quickly by agreeing with Lior
She plans to rewrite Lior's conclusion without telling him
explanation: By admitting she should have spoken up sooner, Maya concedes that not voicing her concerns earlier — and instead silently redoing Lior's work — helped create the resentment and miscommunication.
wrongReasons: A: she does not accept full responsibility; Lior's lateness is also acknowledged
C: the conversation continues productively with a full plan, suggesting genuine agreement
D: no intention to rewrite silently again is implied; she agrees to review, not rewrite

text: What is the lecture mainly about?
choices: The reasons coral reefs are declining worldwide
How coral reefs support marine biodiversity
Methods scientists use to restore damaged reefs
The economic value of reef tourism
explanation: The professor states at the outset that the lecture covers 'why coral reefs are disappearing,' and every section explains a cause of reef decline.
wrongReasons: B: biodiversity is mentioned as context but is not the main topic
C: restoration methods are not discussed
D: tourism economics are not addressed

passage.title: Lecture: Why Coral Reefs Are Disappearing
passage.body: Professor: Coral reefs cover less than one percent of the ocean floor, yet they support roughly a quarter of all marine species. Today we're asking why they're disappearing. The primary driver is ocean warming caused by climate change. Corals live in a narrow temperature range, and when water gets too warm, they expel the algae living in their tissues — a process called bleaching. Without that algae, the coral loses its colour and its main food source. If temperatures remain elevated, the coral dies. The second major threat is ocean acidification: as the ocean absorbs atmospheric CO₂, it becomes more acidic, which weakens the calcium carbonate skeletons corals build. Then there are localised threats — overfishing disrupts the ecosystems reefs depend on, coastal development causes sedimentation that blocks sunlight, and agricultural runoff creates algal blooms that smother corals. The combination of these stressors is devastating. In the past thirty years, roughly half of the world's coral reefs have been lost. Scientists warn that without significant reductions in greenhouse gas emissions, most remaining reefs will be gone by 2050.

text: What happens to coral during a bleaching event?
choices: The coral skeleton dissolves due to increased ocean acidity
The coral expels its algae, losing colour and its main food source
The coral is smothered by sediment from coastal construction
The coral is overgrown by algae due to nutrient runoff
explanation: The professor explains bleaching directly: corals 'expel the algae living in their tissues,' lose colour, and lose 'their main food source.'
wrongReasons: A: skeleton dissolution is a separate process (acidification), not bleaching
C: sedimentation is a different localised threat
D: algal blooms smothering are a separate threat, the opposite of bleaching

text: What is the professor's tone when presenting the statistic about reef loss over the past thirty years?
choices: Cautiously optimistic — emphasising that half still remain
Alarmed — using it to stress the severity of the crisis
Detached — treating it as a neutral scientific observation
Sceptical — questioning the accuracy of the figure
explanation: The statistic follows the word 'devastating' and leads directly into a warning about 2050, clearly framing it as evidence of a crisis rather than a neutral data point.
wrongReasons: A: the professor does not frame the remaining reefs optimistically
C: words like 'devastating' and the 2050 warning signal engagement, not detachment
D: no doubt is cast on the statistic

text: How does the professor organise the threats to coral reefs?
choices: From least to most damaging
Alphabetically by category
From global climate-driven threats to localised human activities
Chronologically from oldest to most recent threat
explanation: The lecture presents ocean warming and acidification — both global, climate-driven — first (labelled 'primary' and 'second major'), then shifts to 'localised threats': overfishing, coastal development, and agricultural runoff.
wrongReasons: A: severity ranking is not the stated organising principle
B: alphabetical order is not used
D: no dates or timeline are given for when each threat emerged

text: What is the main goal of the tutorial session?
choices: To help the student understand what statistical significance means
To teach the student how to use statistical software
To review the student's entire research methodology
To explain the difference between mean and median
explanation: The tutor opens by asking what the student knows about statistical significance, and the whole session is devoted to explaining and clarifying that concept.
wrongReasons: B: software is not mentioned
C: only one aspect of methodology — significance — is covered
D: mean and median are not discussed

passage.title: Tutorial: Understanding Statistical Significance
passage.body: Tutor: Okay, your results section uses the phrase 'statistically significant' several times. Can you tell me what that actually means? Student: It means the result is important? Tutor: That's the most common misconception. Statistical significance does not mean importance — it means the result is unlikely to be due to chance alone. Specifically, when we say a result is significant at p less than 0.05, we mean there is less than a five percent probability that we would see this result if there were actually no real effect. Student: Oh. So a significant result could still be a small effect? Tutor: Exactly. A study with a huge sample size can find a statistically significant difference of, say, one millimetre in height between two groups. That's real, not chance — but it's practically meaningless. That's why we also report effect size. Student: What's effect size? Tutor: It's a measure of how large or meaningful the difference actually is — things like Cohen's d or r. Significance tells you the result is probably real; effect size tells you whether it matters. Student: I've been using 'significant' to mean important this whole time. Tutor: Most people do. Go back through your results section and make sure you're using it correctly.

text: According to the tutor, what does a p-value of less than 0.05 indicate?
choices: There is less than a 5% chance that the result is due to chance alone
The result is five times more important than average
The sample size was large enough to detect a real effect
The effect size is at least 0.05 standard deviations
explanation: The tutor states explicitly: 'p less than 0.05 means there is less than a five percent probability that we would see this result if there were actually no real effect.'
wrongReasons: B: importance is the misconception being corrected
C: sample size adequacy is not what p-value measures
D: p-value and effect size are different statistics

text: How does the tutor feel about the student's misuse of the word 'significant'?
choices: Surprised — it is a rare error among students
Unsympathetic — the student should have known this already
Understanding — the tutor acknowledges it is a very common mistake
Indifferent — the tutor changes the subject quickly
explanation: When the student admits misusing the term, the tutor responds 'most people do,' normalising the error and showing understanding rather than criticism.
wrongReasons: A: the tutor says 'most people' make this error, implying it is common not rare
B: no criticism or impatience is expressed
D: the tutor immediately gives constructive correction, not indifference

text: What is the implicit lesson the tutor conveys through the one-millimetre height example?
choices: Large sample sizes should be avoided in research
A result can be statistically real yet have no practical importance
Height differences are the easiest way to illustrate statistics
Statistical software often produces errors with large datasets
explanation: The tutor uses the absurd example of a one-millimetre height difference that is statistically significant but 'practically meaningless' to show that statistical significance alone does not indicate practical or scientific importance.
wrongReasons: A: large sample sizes are not criticised as a method
C: height is used as an arbitrary example, not because it is specially suited
D: software errors are not mentioned
